coredata.dc:title: Insights into the uses of two azine decorated d10-MOFs for corrosion inhibition application on mild steel surface in saline medium: Experimental as well as theoretical investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Mandal, Sukdeb
Bej, Sourav
Banerjee, Priyabrata
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the use of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) as an efficient corrosion inhibitor has been focused on due to their unique excellent properties such as being rich in π-electron system, chemical and thermal robustness and supramolecular task-specific functionality etc. The present work demonstrates the judicious synthesis of two d10-MOFs namely CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF utilizing organic struts like 4,4′-oxybisbenzoic acid (OBA) and (1E,2E)-1,2-bis(1-(pyridine-4-yl)ethylidene)hydrazine (BPD) and investigated as efficient inhibitor for mild steel corrosion in an adverse corrosive medium i.e. in 3.5 wt% saline medium. The applications of MOFs as heterogenous inhibitor for mild steel corrosion in saline medium have been assessed via potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies. The EIS analysis shows that the effectiveness of corrosion inhibition rises with increasing inhibitor concentrations, reaching 90.15 % with the application of 50 ppm of CI@Zn-MOF. PDP study reveals that both MOFs act as a mixed kind of inhibitor. Post-corrosion study of the mild steel surface has been characterised by Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis and 3D-surface profilometer study demonstrating that the application of inhibitors results in a significant reduction of the corrosion process. The water contact angles on the steel surface increased from 49° to 104.2° and 108.1° due to the adsorption of inhibitors on the steel surface following the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model. Furthermore insightful explanations and correlations with density functional theory (DFT) have been provided for the experimentally observed corrosion inhibition performance as well as adsorption capability. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation has been conducted to mimic the interactions of the inhibitors with metal surface atoms and corrosive species in an actual corrosive medium. The high binding energy (1328.12 kJ/mol for CI@Zn-MOF) provides evidence that inhibitors are able to spontaneously adsorb on steel surfaces. Additionally, the pivotal role of non-covalent intermolecular interactions in a confined space has been assessed using non-covalent interaction based on the reduced density gradient (NCI-RDG) technique.

head.ce:title: Insights into the uses of two azine decorated d10-MOFs for corrosion inhibition application on mild steel surface in saline medium: Experimental as well as theoretical investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electric Mobility and Tribology Research Group, CSIR-Central Mechanical Engineering Research Institute, Mahatma Gandhi Avenue, Durgapur 713209, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the use of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) as an efficient corrosion inhibitor has been focused on due to their unique excellent properties such as being rich in π-electron system, chemical and thermal robustness and supramolecular task-specific functionality etc. The present work demonstrates the judicious synthesis of two d10-MOFs namely CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF utilizing organic struts like 4,4′-oxybisbenzoic acid (OBA) and (1E,2E)-1,2-bis(1-(pyridine-4-yl)ethylidene)hydrazine (BPD) and investigated as efficient inhibitor for mild steel corrosion in an adverse corrosive medium i.e. in 3.5 wt% saline medium. The applications of MOFs as heterogenous inhibitor for mild steel corrosion in saline medium have been assessed via potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies. The EIS analysis shows that the effectiveness of corrosion inhibition rises with increasing inhibitor concentrations, reaching 90.15 % with the application of 50 ppm of CI@Zn-MOF. PDP study reveals that both MOFs act as a mixed kind of inhibitor. Post-corrosion study of the mild steel surface has been characterised by Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis and 3D-surface profilometer study demonstrating that the application of inhibitors results in a significant reduction of the corrosion process. The water contact angles on the steel surface increased from 49° to 104.2° and 108.1° due to the adsorption of inhibitors on the steel surface following the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model. Furthermore insightful explanations and correlations with density functional theory (DFT) have been provided for the experimentally observed corrosion inhibition performance as well as adsorption capability. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation has been conducted to mimic the interactions of the inhibitors with metal surface atoms and corrosive species in an actual corrosive medium. The high binding energy (1328.12 kJ/mol for CI@Zn-MOF) provides evidence that inhibitors are able to spontaneously adsorb on steel surfaces. Additionally, the pivotal role of non-covalent intermolecular interactions in a confined space has been assessed using non-covalent interaction based on the reduced density gradient (NCI-RDG) technique.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion has been identified as one of the most serious concerns in various sectors because it accounts for a significant amount of the operating and maintenance expenses of several industrial processes and causes huge annual economic losses [1–3]. The steel-based amenities of marine engineering are vulnerable to general, localized and galvanic erosion, which has become a major concern in terms of economic loss, potential environmental impact, and safety concerns. The Corrosiveness of the marine environment depends on several factors, wherein the saline media is known to play the key role. It has been reported that 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions are generally used as a reference for mimicking saline condition simulation in the laboratory [4–6]. Among the various adopted process to safeguard the steel surfaces against corrosion namely cathodic, anodic protection and the application of different corrosion inhibitors. The application of corrosion inhibitors has gained much attention owing to its more realistic, cost-effective, and efficient procedure to control corrosion in metals [7–10]. Inhibitors function as shielding fence that directly prevents the interaction of the metal surfaces with the corrosive environment, thereby reducing the rate of corrosion [11–14].

Different heteroatoms, electron donor sites, and π-electron moieties containing a diverse class of inorganic and organic compounds have long been used as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in different corrosive mediums [10,15–20]. It has been found that many organic compounds that contain nitrogen, sulphur, and oxygen-like heteroatoms and aromatic moieties are capable of acting as corrosion inhibitors (CI) on mild steel in a wide range of aggressive corrosive media [10,15–20]. Adsorption of inhibitors to metal surfaces is possible by physisorption, chemisorption, or both [15–20]. In a number of studies, organic, inorganic, and natural corrosion-inhibiting products have been shown to be effective as corrosion inhibitors by utilizing a variety of methods. The inclusion of heteroatoms with high electron densities (N, S, O, and P) and unpaired electrons that mostly interact with metal atoms' unoccupied d-orbitals makes organic CIs excellent for this purpose. Additionally, it has been observed that CIs with heterocyclic aromatic groups, such as imidazole, pyridine, pyrrolidone, and thiazole, among others, are resistant to corrosion when combined with polar functional groups like primary amines (NH2), secondary amines (NH) and carbonyl groups (CO) whose double bonds enhance the interaction with the metallic surface [21–23]. However, the use of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) materials as organic–inorganic hybrid corrosion inhibitors for mild steel surface protection has received little attention to date [24]. MOFs are organic–inorganic hybrid materials that depend on metal ions to form coordination bonds with organic ligands that are especially suited to a given target, or more precisely secondary building blocks (SBUs) [25,26]. These materials are subsequently characterised following surface area classification and uniform pore size distribution which ultimately helps in searching out the profound application either in sensing, [27] gas storage, [28] separation, [29] removal, [30] catalysis, [31] anti-counterfeit application, [32] drug delivery [33] etc.

The main characteristics of MOFs, such as their large specific surface area, flexible structure and function, uniform pore size distribution, abundance of active sites and chemical and thermal robustness make them excellent candidates for use as anti-corrosion materials. MOF materials have infinite 1D, 2D or 3D network structures, specific surface area, and uniform pore size distribution, adsorption affinity allowing it easy to adsorb on metal surfaces and inhibit corrosion by forming a protective layer [34,35]. Less hazardous, bio-benign properties of the MOFs make them the potential next-generation corrosion inhibitor substances. In a reported research work the inhibitory application of zeolitic imidazolate framework material (ZIF-8@{Mo132}) was explored in HCl solution and it was observed that the presence of ionic sites on the ZIF-8@{Mo132} cluster’s surface which allow Mo132 to effectively occupy the ZIF-8 surface, resulting in better performance [36]. The Keplerate-like polyoxometalate [Mo132] was present in the solutions as ions, and it disintegrated in aqueous solution into a negative-charged cluster. Another research work explained the application of two silver (Ag) based MOFs as CI for copper in an HCl solution [35]. According to the experimental findings, both MOFs function as mixed-type inhibitors and physically adhere on the copper surface in accordance with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The corrosion inhibition performances of a Cd-MOF viz. [Cd(SCN)2(6-mquin)2] based on cadmium thiocyanate and 6-methylequinoline, for the copper surface was investigated in HCl medium [37]. Ambidentate SCN-, one of the potential bridging ligands for the formation of MOFs, was present in the system. The incorporation of d10-metal ions in metal–organic systems is also of immense interest because of their fascinating physical properties. In recent times cadmium(II)-containing complexes have attracted much interest owing to the ability of Cd to form bonds simultaneously with different donor sites, resulting in the birth of various coordination modes, as well as colourful physiochemical properties of Cd(II) ion.

It has been observed that in previously reported research works; the inhibitory application of MOFs-based corrosion inhibitors has mostly been studied in acid and/or neutral solutions. MOFs have been less extensively studied when it comes to their performance to inhibit corrosion in saline media [38]. The principal object of this present investigation is to develop a low-cost user affable d10-MOF-based high-performance (in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium) corrosion inhibitor in a simplistic way and to inspect its impeding nature against corrosion on mild steel surfaces on exposure to the aforementioned corrosive medium. Getting inspired by the interesting features of the MOF network, herein, the corrosion inhibition performances of two MOFs namely CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF have been investigated in a simulated saline medium. The bottom-up approach of MOFs by selecting specific metals and organic ligands enables these materials to possess desired structures and functions. Additionally, the protection of the iron surface is aided by the presence of more electropositive metals, such as zinc. It is also important to note that the effectiveness of sacrificial inhibition is increased by the addition of more electropositive metals to the organic framework [35,39]. Fourier-transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and powder X-ray diffraction analysis have all been used to determine the structure of the inhibitors. TGA analysis has been employed to determine the inhibitors' thermal stability. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) have been used to analyse the electrochemical performance; surface analysis, including field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM), have applied to evaluate the surface characteristics of the mild steel before and after immersion. By measuring the water contact angle, the hydrophobic behaviour of the surface following the adsorption of the inhibitors has been identified. Furthermore, theoretical studies such as density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) simulation have been performed to corroborate the experimental findings. The weak interactions present in inhibitor molecules, such as H-bonds, Van der Waals, steric repulsion, etc., have also been identified using non-covalent interaction analysis based on reduced density gradient (NCI-RDG).
All the chemicals and solvents used in the present investigation were of analytical grades, supplied by Sigma Aldrich (now Merck, India); and used without any further purification.

The two inhibitors i.e. CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF were synthesised by a solvothermal synthesis process which was reported earlier [29]. At first 4,4-oxybisbenzoic acid (25.6 mg, 0.1 mmol) and (1E,2E)-1,2-bis(1-(pyridine-4-yl)ethylidene)hydrazine (22 mg, 0.1 mmol) were dissolved in 6 mL mixture of dimethyl formamide (DMF) and chlorobenzene at 4:1 ratio. Thereafter 30 mg (0.1 mmol) Cd(NO3)2·4H2O was added and the final mixture was sonicated properly. After filtration, the solution was kept in a hot air oven at 120 °C for 72 h. At the end, yellow-coloured products were obtained and adequately cleaned with DMF solvent.

Similarly, CI@Zn-MOF i.e. [Zn(OBA)(BPD)]n was also synthesised via the same solvothermal process. 25.6 mg (0.1 mmol) of 4,4′-oxybisbenzoic acid and 22 mg (0.1 mmol) of (1E,2E)-1,2-bis(1-(pyridine-4-yl)ethylidene)hydrazine were dissolved in 6 mL DMF solvent. After the completion of the reaction orange coloured crystals were obtained. The final material was washed with DMF. A schematic synthesis procedure of the CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF has been presented in Scheme 1
.

Using the Spectrum 100 FT-IR spectrometer from Perkin Elmer, the FT-IR spectra of the synthesized inhibitors were acquired in the 4000–450 cm−1 region. Raman analyses were performed with an RS Raman spectrometer (WITec Alpha 300) using a 532 nm laser as the excitation source. PXRD analyses of the synthesised inhibitors were performed by utilizing an X-ray diffractometer (Make: PANalytical X- pert PRO, 2θ range of 5°–60° with a scan rate of 10°/min) to assess the crystallinity of the synthetic materials. The thermal stability of the synthesized corrosion inhibitors was analysed by a thermogravimetric analyser (STA 449 F1 Jupiter, with a temperature ramp 10 K/min).

Commercially available mild steel specimen (composition C: 0.22 wt%, Si: 0.31 wt%, Mn: 0.60 wt%, P 0.04 wt%, and S: 0.06 wt% and the remainder is Fe) was used in this work. In order to get a finely polished surface, metallurgical grade (P400-1600) emery paper was used to abrade the surface after polishing it using a twin disc polishing equipment. The finely polished sample was then washed with distilled water and acetone to clean the surface. 3.5 wt% NaCl solution as simulated seawater was prepared by adding analytical grade NaCl (Merck India) to distilled H2O and was used as a corrosive medium for all the experimentations. First, the two inhibitor materials were finely powdered. Next, the powder material was thoroughly mixed with 3.5 wt% of NaCl solution by ultrasonic vibration to prepare the test sample solution at four different concentration, namely 5 ppm, 10 ppm, 25 ppm, and 50 ppm.

A three-electrode corrosion flat cell (volume capacity 250 mL) equipped with a platinum gauze counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode as a reference electrode, and a mild steel sample (exposed area 1 cm2) used as the working electrode in a potentiostat/galvanostat (PARSTAT 3000A, AMETEK, USA) was used for the electrochemical experiments, such as the PDP technique and EIS. Open circuit potential (OCP) was monitored for 45 min to achieve the system’s stability. The potential vs time plot has been displayed in Fig. S1 of the supplemental information files and which demonstrates that the experimental setup has reached equilibrium. After establishing a steady state potential, EIS and PDP experiments were carried out.

All the PDP studies were carried out in 3.5 % NaCl solution at room temperature (27 °C) within a potential range of ± 250 mV with respect to OCP with a scan rate of 0.16 mV/s [40,41]. The polarization approach was used to assess the corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion current density (icorr), cathodic slope (βc), and anodic slope (βa), to monitor the corrosion kinetics. These parameters are crucial for examining the corrosion-inhibitive behaviour of the inhibitors. The anodic and cathodic curves were extrapolated towards corrosion potential (Ecorr) and the intersection points of cathodic anodic slopes indicate corrosion current density (icorr).

The percentage of surface coverage (θPDP) and the inhibition percentage (η%PDP) derived from the PDP analysis have been calculated using the following formulae [42,43].

The EIS studies were carried out by altering the current amplitude of ± 10 mV/rms within the frequency range 100 kHz to 10 mHz [42]. According to an equivalent circuit model, the solution resistance is represented by Rs while charge transfer resistance is designated by Rct and the CPE stands for constant phase element, as a substitute of electric double-layer capacitors. Surface morphological alterations of mild steel surfaces exposed to a corrosive environment i.e., 3.5 wt% NaCl medium without and with inhibitor were studied using FE-SEM analysis (ZEISS Sigma HD). Elemental analysis was performed by an EDX spectrometer equipped with the FE-SEM instrument. The FE-SEM and EDX analyses were conducted on mild steel samples without and with inhibitors (highest concentration) in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium after 8 h of exposure. A non-contact 3D surface profilometer (model Taylor Hobson) was used to investigate surface topography, and all profile parameters were obtained according to the ISO 4287 method [46]. Using a commercial goniometer (Model: Datahysics, Germany), a water droplet's contact angle with mild steel surfaces was evaluated both before and after inhibitors were applied [41].

It is necessary to take into account both the molecular and electrical characteristics of the molecules to demonstrate how corrosion inhibitor molecules can adhere to the metal surface and prevent corrosion. Frontier molecular orbital (FMO) analysis is essential for visualising the potential geometric structure of inhibitors adsorbed on metal surfaces and for evaluating their effectiveness in adhering to metal surfaces even when corrosive species are present. A simulation model sheds light on the procedure by modelling the added CI's adsorption in the corrosive media and explaining the mechanism. In the present investigation, MD simulation methods were employed and the resultant outcomes have been discussed in the relevant section.

All the spin-polarized DFT calculations were performed using Dmol3 modules incorporated in Material StudioTM software programme package [47,48]. The optimizations of geometry and electronic properties were carried out using generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Perdew-Burke-Ernzernhof (PBE) functional. Double numerical polarization (DNP) basis sets were employed for all atoms [49].

Following the identification of the FMOs, namely the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), the energy of HOMO (EHOMO) the energy of LUMO (ELUMO), the absolute electronegativity (χ) and hardness (η) as well as the overall reactivities of the molecule, were evaluated. Pearson equation has been adopted to calculate the electronegativity (χ) and hardness (η).

Non-covalent interaction analysis (NCI) is a valuable tool to visualise and investigate weak interactions occurring in three dimensions [50,51]. This model is based on reduced density gradients (RDGs) and electron densities (q) expressed this way:

Where, electron density represents by

.

At particular distances, weak non-covalent interactions such as the H-bond, van der Waals interaction, and steric repulsions take place. The associative effects of these weak interactions may manifest remarkable changes [52]. Using the Multiwfn programme, in the present study, NCI based on the RDG were examined to observe the weak interactions present in the system [53]. Visual molecular dynamics (VMD) interface was used to visualise and extract the outcomes. Additionally, gnuplot was also used to plot the colour scatter graphs of the organic components [54].

In order to examine the precise arrangement of the adsorbed inhibitor on the metal surface and ascertain the interaction energy, MD simulation is a very helpful technique [55]. In this current research work the MD simulation was executed in Material studioTM software (V6.1) [47,56]. By utilizing the COMPASS forcefield in the Forcite module, the inhibitor molecules, water, sodium, and chloride ions were initially geometrically optimized. Following that, a solution slab containing an inhibitor, 150 numbers of H2O molecules and 15 numbers of each sodium and chloride ions was built for each system. Thereafter geometry optimization of the constructed solution slab was performed. Instead of Fe(100) or Fe(101) and other surfaces, Fe(110) surface was chosen for this experiment because it has a densely packed structure and is energetically more stable [57–59].

After that, to create a simulation model of 39.82 × 39.82 × 76.92 Å3 the optimised solution slab was positioned above the Fe(110) surface while leaving a 10 Å vacuum slab as a blank space at the top layer. Before running the MD simulation, the geometry of the built-in simulation model was tuned to prevent undesirable molecule configurations and produce an energy-optimal simulation setup. The stabilisation of the energy and temperature fluctuation curves proved that the optimization approach was effective. The MD simulation was carried out using the Forcite module of Material StudioTM using the COMPASS force field and NVT ensemble with a time step of 1 fs for 200 ps at 298 K [47].

The MD simulation results show that the chosen inhibitor molecule spontaneously interacts with metal surface’s atoms in the vicinity of corrosive species including H2O, Na+, and Cl-. ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR, RAMAN, PXRD and TGA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The molecular structure of the synthesised MOFs was characterised by FT-IR spectra. The recorded FTIR spectra of the synthesised inhibitors have been presented in Fig. S2. The absence of a prominent absorption band at 1685 cm−1 in both inhibitors indicates that full deprotonation of the carboxylic group of OBA occurs after coordination with the metal ions. Furthermore, it provides evidence that the system does not contain free COOH group. The asymmetric stretching frequencies of the carboxylate group in CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF inhibitor appeared at 1594 and 1588 cm−1 respectively and symmetric stretching frequencies appeared at 1387 and 1394 cm−1 respectively. The FTIR spectral data are consistent with the RAMAN and PXRD analysis [62]. The Raman analysis (ranging from 100 to 2000 cm−1) was done at room temperature to validate the production of the synthesised inhibitors (vide Fig. S3 ). OBA ligands contain a CC group in the aromatic rings, which results in the band at 1600 cm−1, whereas the band at 1500 cm−1 arises from the stretching of CO. It has been observed that the aromatic ring's CH component exhibits a band at 660 cm−1 as a result of its out-of-plane deformation mode. The band for ether linkage (-COC-) of the OBA ligand present in the MOF appears at 864 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 (asymmetric stretch) while a relatively small band at 425 cm−1 for the Cd-O bond reveals the successful formation of the CI@Cd-MOF. Another peak appears at 440 cm−1 in the case of CI@Zn-MOF which is absent in the case of CI@Cd-MOF [63,64]. PXRD patterns of the two inhibitors (vide Fig. S4) have been well corroborated with the simulated peaks, which signify the successful formation of the synthesised inhibitors. TGA analysis (vide Fig. S5) of the studied inhibitors demonstrated that both inhibitors have very good thermal stability.

The impedance in the low-frequency region (0.01 Hz) is inversely correlated with the rate of corrosion, as shown in Bode plots (vide Fig. 2 b and 3b) and an increase in impedance with concentration demonstrates that the inhibitors' ability to inhibit corrosion is more effective as their concentration increases [6,76]. Significantly, the
Z
demonstrated an upward trend in correlation with the increase in inhibitor concentration. The formation of a protective layer on the surface of mild steel was demonstrated by the
Z
value which was 200 Ω cm2 in the absence of an inhibitor and ∼ 1000 Ω cm2 and ∼ 2000 Ω cm2 respectively with the addition of 50 ppm of CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF. These findings demonstrated that by preventing direct contact between the corrosive medium and mild steel surface, the CI@Zn-MOF could exhibit exceptional corrosion prevention for mild steelin a saline medium.

The PDP method is an experimental technique for finding out the kinetics of corrosion and monitoring the corrosion reaction on the metal's surface. The resultant Tafel plot from PDP analysis for the highest concentration (50 ppm) of the inhibitors in the studied corrosive medium has been presented in Fig. 4
.

The obtained order of icorr values from the Fig. 4 signifies the greater adsorption of CI@Zn-MOF followed by CI@Cd-MOF. Inhibitor molecules' ability to bind the surfaces is inherent to their molecular architecture. Typically, organic molecules with aromatic moieties and azomethine linkages are readily adsorbed on the surface of the metal, and the inhibitor molecules subsequently function as a corrosion-inhibiting agent [82,83].

As a result of the addition of MOF inhibitors to the corrosive medium, more cathodic polarizations were observed, resulting in a significant decrease in corrosion current density. The results are in good agreement with the EIS measurements.

Adsorption isotherms are widely recognised for providing important insight into the corrosion inhibition process. The added inhibitors in corrosive solution interact with the exposed surface of the metal and subsequently get adsorbed by dislocating water molecules adhered to the surface. Water molecules have been replaced by inhibitor molecules through a quasi-substitution process as inhibitor molecules adhere to the electrode surface [84].
(18)
Inhibitor
solution
+
x
H 2
O
adsorbed
↔
Inhibitor
adsorbed
+
x
H 2
O
solution

Where Inhibitorsolution and Inhibitoradsorbed indicate the inhibitor molecules present in saline medium and those are adsorbed on the metal surface respectively. As usual, the terms H2Osolution and H2Oadsorbed denote the availability of water molecules in saline media and on the electrode surface respectively. Where, x is the number of displaced water molecules. Adsorption of inhibitor takes place at the interface of the metal surface and solution and forms a thin barrier on the metal's exposed surface and acts as a protective layer that protects the metal surface from the aggressive attack of the corrosive medium. The degree of surface coverage (θ) by inhibitors determines whether corrosion inhibition efficiency improves or decreases. Surface coverage (θ) has been plotted against inhibitor concentration (C) to obtain the adsorption isotherm. It is possible to demonstrate how an inhibitor interacts with a mild steel surface using several isotherm models, including Langmuir isotherms, Frumkin isotherms, Flory-Huggins isotherms, Temkin isotherms, and Freundlich isotherms.

It has been determined that only the Langmuir adsorption isotherm is capable of accurately fitting the experimental data resulting from the EIS experiment as depicted in Fig. 5 . Whether the other adsorption isotherms i.e. Frendluich, Frumkin, Flory-Huggins and Temkin isotherm which are not fitted linearly have been shown in Fig. S6 . As a result, the inhibitor molecules adhere next to one another and do not display lateral interactions, leading to the creation of a consistent monolayer.

A liner correlation describing C and θ that is consistent with the Langmuir isotherm is expressed as:

Where, Kads is the equilibrium adsorption constant.
Furthermore, the calculated Kads are in the order: CI@Zn-MOF > CI@Cd-MOF, which confirms strong adsorption of CI@Zn-MOF followed by CI@Cd-MOF.

The adsorption free energy (ΔG0 ads), in addition to the Kads values, is one of the key factors in understanding the inhibitors' spontaneous adsorption.

have been recorded in Table 3 . The adsorption of inhibitors is frequently controlled by the electrostatic interaction between charged surface atoms and charged inhibitors, when the values of

are near to −20 kJ/mol or less negative; this type of adsorption is known as physisorption and then it is manifested by electrostatic interactions. If the value of
Δ

In Table 3 , the calculated

0

As a result, the adsorption of the inhibitors onto the metal surface is strengthened by their dual adsorption nature, i.e., physisorption and chemisorption.

Additionally, the excess electrons in the iron centre can also be transferred back to the suitable π-orbital of the inhibitor, enabling the back donation. As a result, inhibitors are rendered more effective at adsorbing metal surfaces, forming an intact protective film able to retard oxidative degradation at metal surfaces.

The morphology of the exposed surfaces of the mild steel samples into 3.5 wt% NaCl medium with and without inhibitors were studied using FE-SEM analysis. The corresponding elemental analysis was analysed using EDX analysis. Mild steel samples were immersed for 8 h in corrosive saline solution without and with inhibitor at the highest concentration and then analyzed by FE-SEM and EDX. All the FE-SEM images and corresponding EDX data have been presented in Fig. 6
. According to the results of the FE-SEM analysis, the mild steel’s surface exposed to pure NaCl medium, that is, without inhibitor, suffers more damage as a result of the salt solution's corrosive character and many pitting spots have been seen on the exposed surface. In addition, the tiny holes indicate the starting of more pitting, which might proceed if exposed for a long time. However, the surface of the mild steel substrate was not significantly damaged by the corrosive medium when the corrosion inhibitor was added to the corrosive medium. From the Fig. 6 c it has been attributed that the addition of CI@Zn-MOF in corrosive medium occurs less damage to the mild steel surface. Therefore, more surface coverage results from the adsorption of CI@Zn-MOF, which has stronger surface protection capabilities compared to CI@Cd-MOF.

According to the EDX spectra analysis, there are no N and O peaks for the mild steel taken from 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without inhibitors. On the other hand, the presence of N and O elemental peaks in the inhibitor-containing 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions has been explained by the adsorption of the inhibitors onto the surface of mild steel. Hence, it has been demonstrated using FE-SEM and EDX analysis that the inhibitors have adsorbed on the surface and have decreased mild steel's corrosion-related degradation. FE-SEM and EDX data also support the order of inhibition performance obtained from EIS as well as PDP measurements of the studied inhibitors CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF.

A non-contact optical 3D surface profilometer was used to analyse the mild steel surface's topography and surface roughness parameter; the resulting pictures have been displayed in Fig. 7
. 3D surface profilometer measurement gives an idea about average roughness (Sa) and square roughness (Sq) of the assessed surface. Herein the roughness values of the surface are high in the absence of inhibitors and decreased in the presence of inhibitors indicating that the inhibitor molecules act as a barrier layer on the mild steel surface [87,88].

The roughness parameter values calculated as per ISO 4287 standard method has tabulated in Table S1 . The roughness characteristics of CI@Zn-MOF are considerably lower than those of both blank mild steel and CI@Cd-MOF, demonstrating that CI@Zn-MOF has a greater ability to create protective films than either blank mild steel or CI@Cd-MOF. In presence of a corrosive medium i.e. in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution the mild steel surface becomes very uneven and bumpy. The arithmetic mean deviation of the assessed profile (Ra) and maximum peak height (Rz) have been 0.23 µm and 3.18 µm respectively. Again, surface roughness values significantly decreased in the presence of inhibitors. The arithmetic mean deviation of the assessed profile (Ra) has been 0.18 µm and 0.11 µm and the maximum peak height (Rz) has been 2.45 µm and 2.01 µm for CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF respectively. The obtained findings support FE-SEM and EDX analyses and illustrate that CI@Zn-MOF has a stronger capability to prevent corrosion.

Recently, contact angle measurement has been utilized to compare the mild steel surface'snature and corrosion inhibition characteristics. A substance's hydrophobicity is associated with its anticorrosive properties. The nature of the hydrophobic or hydrophilic behaviour of the surface can easily be understood from water contact angle analysis. The corresponding water contact angle of the steel sample after 8 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with and without the addition of CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF have been shown in Fig. 8
. The measured contact angle for the blank surface is 49° ± 2°, after addition of CI@Cd-MOF is 104° ± 2°, and CI@Zn-MOF is 108° ± 2°. The addition of inhibitors causes a hydrophobic film to grow on the mild steel surface, which enables the surface to become hydrophobic. The adsorption of CI@Zn-MOF on the mild steel surface the contact angle becomes higher and the surface becomes more hydrophobic. These findings demonstrate that the addition of MOFs can stop surface corrosion by creating a shielding layer on the surface. In comparison, CI@Zn-MOF shows greater inhibition performance than CI@Cd-MOF.

The corrosion inhibition performance of CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF involves the adsorption process on the mild steel surface. Organic molecules may adsorb at the interface between metals and solutions in three different ways (i) A charged metal atom electrostatically attracted to a charged molecule, (ii) Unshared pair of electrons in inhibitor molecules interact with the metal surface, (iii) Interaction of pi-electrons with the metal. The inhibition effects depend upon the geometry, size, and chain length of an organic ligand and also the concentration of the inhibitor. Herein the order of inhibition efficiency is as follows CI@Zn-MOF > CI@Cd-MOF. Both the MOFs consisted of BPD and 4,4ˊ-oxybisbenzoic acid as they contain donor atoms like N and O which help towards the adsorption of the MOFs on the metal surface. The inhibition efficiency has been strongly affected by the adsorption of the molecule on the surface and which depends on the electron density around the donor centres. With increasing electron density around the donor centres the degree of adsorption increases and followed by inhibition efficiency increases. The oxygen atoms present in the 4,4ˊ-oxybisbenzoic acid ligand interact with the empty d-orbital of the iron atom and strongly adsorb by the chemisorptions process and prevent the greater number of surface are to come into contact with the corrosive media. Here CI@Zn-MOF shows higher inhibition efficiency than CI@Cd-MOF due to the presence of an electropositive Zn centre in CI@Zn-MOF. It is important to note that the efficacy of sacrificial inhibition rises with the addition of more electropositive metals to the organic framework, as zinc and other electropositive metals aid to protect iron compounds. In addition to that easy synthetic procedures and excellent corrosion inhibition performance even in very low concentrations make these MOFs superior corrosion inhibitors in their congener (vide Table S2).

DFT is increasingly being used as a tool to develop potential corrosion inhibitors based on quantum chemical parameters. We can evaluate the reactivity of the created inhibitors on metal surfaces by looking at the electron density distribution on the inhibitors' molecular orbitals. The ability for molecules to donate electrons is associated with HOMO, whereas the ability to accept electrons is associated with LUMO. By transferring their electrons to the metalsurface's empty d-orbitals, an electron-donating inhibitor molecule binds with Fe(110) to create a coordinate-type bond. For the DFT calculation, the simplified structure of both the inhibitors CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF were analysed here. The optimized geometry, HOMO and LUMO of the inhibitors have been presented in Fig. 9
. The frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) have the most significant role in predicting how organic compounds would interact with other reactive substances. The energy of the HOMO (EHOMO) and the LUMO (ELUMO) have been considered in order to analyse the chemical reactivity of a molecule [49,89]. The EHOMO is connected with the donating ability of a molecule. Inhibitor molecules that exhibit a high EHOMO value are more capable of electron donation to metal surfaces, thereby facilitating stronger adsorption [49]. Moreover, ELUMO of inhibitor molecules is associated with electron accommodating capability. An electron-accepting capacity of a molecule increases with lower ELUMO values. A more facilitated adsorption process leads to lower ELUMO values. According to the calculated quantum chemical data (vide Table 4
) the EHOMO value for the CI@Zn-MOF is higher, indicating that it is more capable of donating electrons than the CI@Cd-MOF. In addition to that also from the ELUMO values it has been seen the following order CI@Zn-MOF < CI@Cd-MOF, thus CI@Zn-MOF is a better electron acceptor, which also supports the experimental results of corrosion inhibition. Apart from the EHOMO and ELUMO another important parameter is the energy gap (ΔE) of HOMO and LUMO (i.e.
Δ E =
E
LUMO
-
E
HOMO
) to assess the reactivity of chemical components and also to determine the adsorption behaviour of the studied inhibitors. With increasing ΔE value the reactivity of the molecules decreases. Thus the system which has less ΔE value the adsorption capacity increases. From Table 4 it has been clearly understood that the ΔE values follow the order CI@Zn-MOF < CI@Cd-MOF. Therefore, a smaller energy gap between HOMO and LUMO will allow electrons to transport more efficiently for CI@Zn-MOF.

HOMO and LUMO are related to ionization potential (I) and electron affinity (A) in molecules according to the Koopman's theorem [49].
(21)

- E
LUMO
Here, the electronegativity of molecules and their overall hardness were calculated using the ionisation potential (I) and electron affinity (A) values. Electronegativity and the global hardness are defined by the equation (19) and (20) respectively.
(23)

(24)

The proportion of electrons exchanged (ΔN) between the inhibitor molecule and the metal surface atoms is a crucial parameter to take into account as it indicates the inhibitory effect of the molecules. This approach explains how electron flow occurs from a lower to a greater electronegative site until the chemical potential reaches equilibrium when the inhibitor molecule and metal surface are close to one another.

The ΔN is estimated using Pearson's equation [49].
(25)

According to recent research, the value of

is theoretically unacceptable since electron–electron interaction was not taken into account, only free electron gas Fermi energy of iron is considered [91–93]. Therefore utilizing the work function (ϕ) rather than the electronegativity (

of the metal surface is more appropriate. Now the new equation of ΔN becomes as follows:

The obtained DFT-derived ϕ value for Fe (110) surface is 4.82 eV [93]. An electron will be transferred from the inhibitor molecule to the metallic surface when ΔN is greater than zero, and vice versa [93]. Awad et al. state that if ΔN< 3.6, the inhibitors' capacity to donate electrons is increased [94].

In this present research work, the calculated ΔN values are greater than zero but less than 3.6 (vide Table 4). As a result, it suggests that the inhibitors have the ability to transfer their electrons to the atoms on the metal surface to aid in its adsorption. In addition, another parameter connected to the reactivity of the inhibitor is the global softness (σ), which is the reciprocal of the global hardness (η) of a molecule. As per Hard-soft acid-base theory hard-hard and soft–soft combinations are the most favourable and stable interaction [95]. Here, organic molecules have been regarded as soft basic centres whereas the iron surface atoms have been regarded as soft acidic centres, favouring the interaction of metal–organic inhibitors.

The non-covalent interactions (NCI) theory is regarded as a cutting-edge theoretical tool used to explain intermolecular interactions and assess the nature of weak interactions of molecular systems. The NCI is a visualization indicator based on density and its derivatives and is coloured according to a strength scale that uses RDG findings at low densities. Usually, the strength is determined by multiplying the electron density (ρ) by the sign of the second-highest eigenvalue (λ2) of the Hessian of the electron density in each point of the isosurface (λ2) which indicates whether the force is attractive or repulsive. If the effective density sign
(
λ 2
) × ρ
is less than zero, the interaction is fundamentally attractive, and H-bonds will form. When the density sign
(
λ 2
) × ρ
is above zero, steric repulsion is present (i.e., non-bonding interactions). Based on the density of the examined organic compounds, the resultant NCI-RDG plots have been shown in Fig. 10
. H-bonding, van der Waals, and steric repulsive interactions are present in the inhibitors are depicted by blue, green, and red colours, respectively, with RDG 0.5 a.u. and sign
(
λ 2
) × ρ
, isovalue within the range −0.035 to 0.020 a.u. This suggests that the electron clouds seen in the dense portions of the recently studied corrosion inhibitors attain stability by giving up their rich electron clouds to the appropriate acceptors. As shown by the scatter diagram in red, steric-repellent interactions can be minimized through their interaction with the metal surfaces that are targeted [52,96].

MD simulation has developed as a cutting-edge approach for studying the interactions of corrosion inhibitors and other corrosive species with metal surfaces [89]. It also aids in visualising the inhibitor molecules' actual adsorption arrangement. The system's energy and temperature fluctuation curves provided evidence that the system successfully attained equilibrium (vide Fig. S7 and Fig. S8). The stable adsorption configurations on the iron surface have been presented in Fig. 11 and Fig. 12 , and Table 5 tabulates the resulting interaction (Einteraction) and binding energy (Ebinding) values obtained from the interaction of the inhibitor with Fe(110) surface. The findings of the MD simulation show that the inhibitor molecules are capable of interacting with the metal surface and then adhering there. The obtained interaction value of the two inhibitors suggests that CI@Zn-MOF interact more efficiently than CI@Cd-MOF. The calculated binding energy follows the order CI@Zn-MOF (1328.12 kJ/mol) > CI@Cd-MOF (1274.27 kJ/mol). MD simulation results revealed that both inhibitors have been adsorbed on the metal surface in a horizontal fashion. The parallel adsorption of the inhibitors has been caused by the interaction of the heteroatomspresent in the inhibitor molecule with Fe(110) surface atoms. There is a greater area of surface covered by the horizontal fashion of adsorption of the inhibitors than by the vertical fashion.

The experimental results and the Einteraction and Ebinding values obtained from MD simulation are well corroborated. The horizontal adsorption orientation becomes feasible by the interaction of iron surface and heteroatoms present in the inhibitor skeleton. Increased surface coverage on the metal surface occurs from the inhibitor's horizontal mode of adsorption. Comparatively, the adsorption of CI@Zn-MOF becomes more feasible than that of CI@Cd-MOF. From a molecular perspective, the CI@Zn-MOF has one unoccupied site, and the inhibitors may adsorb on the metal substrate using that site. On the other hand, the presence of additional electropositive metals, like zinc, aids in the protection of iron compounds; it is significant to emphasise that the addition of more electropositive metals to the organic framework improves the efficiency of sacrificial inhibition. Both these factors make CI@Zn-MOF a potential corrosion inhibitor in the saline medium for the protection of mild steel [35,39].

Additionally, RDF analysis revealed the pathway by which the organic ligands were adsorbed to the iron surface. When the initial peak appears within 3.2 Å, a strong chemical interaction has been confirmed, and when the peak appears between 3.2 Å and 5.0 Å, strong coulomb and van der Waals interactions have been taken into account. Only weak van der Waals forces have been taken into account if the peak emerges beyond 5 Å. The obtained RDF plots have been presented in Figs. 11 and 12 . For the adsorption of both the BPD and OBA on the Fe(110) surface the initially obtained peak lies within 3.2 Å indicating the present nitrogen and oxygen atoms chemically bind to the iron surface [42,61,97,98].

In a nutshell, the current investigation demonstrated the potential corrosion inhibition capabilities of two d10-MOFs viz. CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium using both experimental results followed by theoretical corroboration and the obtained inhibition efficacy following the order CI@Zn-MOF > CI@Cd-MOF. The high charge transfer resistance at the metal electrolyte interface is most likely caused by the adsorption of inhibitors, as determined by the results of the EIS investigation. Even with a rise in inhibitor concentration, the charge transfer process effectively controls the corrosion inhibition process. In comparison to CI@Cd-MOF and CI@Zn-MOF, the latter shows better efficiency this may be due to the following reason, in the CI@Cd-MOF, the Cd-centre is connected to four oxygen atoms of OBA and two nitrogen atoms from BPD and possesses a distorted octahedral geometry. While the Zn centres in CI@Zn-MOF are connected to four oxygen atoms from OBA and one nitrogen atom from the BPD to form pentacoordinated geometry and therefore CI@Zn-MOF facilitates better adsorption on the mild steel surface. Herein, the adsorption process follows Langmuir adsorption isotherms. Furthermore, the negative
Δ
G
ads
0
values demonstrated the spontaneous nature of adsorption. Although the potentiodynamic polarization results revealed that the inhibitors act as mixed-type inhibitors, however, they have suppressed the cathode reactions more than the anode reactions. Surface analysis studies such as FE-SEM, EDX and 3D surface profilometer and water contact angle measurement successfully prove that the mild steel surface is less corroded in the presence of corrosion inhibitors because the inhibitor molecules form a hydrophobic film on the mild steel surface that prevents various corrosive substances from coming into contact with the steel surface. According to a theoretical examination of the molecular fragment's FMOs' energy levels and electron distribution, the inhibitor molecules may be strong enough to bind to the surface of the metal. The order of the simplified unit of the inhibitor molecules' HOMO-LUMO energy gap is CI@Zn-MOF < CI@Cd-MOF. The greater binding energy for CI@Zn-MOF obtained from the MD simulation suggests spontaneous interaction with the metal substrate. The results of the NCI-RDG analysis also indicate that inhibitor molecules have an excellent capability to interact with the metal substrate by using electron-rich sites present in their molecular scaffolds. As a result, the development of novel MOF-based corrosion inhibitors have opened the door to significant advancements in the discipline of corrosion inhibition and these type of inhibitors have the potential to function as the next-generation heterogeneous corrosion inhibitors.
